-{"id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.2","title":"Adopt CompiledRegex for pack patterns","description":"Use the dual-engine regex abstraction so linear-time patterns avoid fancy-regex backtracking overhead.","notes":"Background: packs::regex_engine::CompiledRegex already exists but pack patterns still use fancy_regex everywhere. This is wasted overhead for ~85% of patterns that do not need lookaround/backrefs.

Approach:
- Replace SafePattern/DestructivePattern regex type with CompiledRegex.
- Update safe_pattern!/destructive_pattern! macros to compile through CompiledRegex::new.
- Ensure evaluation uses CompiledRegex::is_match (same semantics).
- Fail-open on compile errors the same way as current code.
- Extend the same dual-engine logic to config overrides (allow/block) where it is safe and semantically identical.

Isomorphism proof: CompiledRegex only selects the linear engine when the pattern syntax is fully supported by regex::Regex, which is semantically equivalent for those patterns. Lookaround/backref patterns still go through fancy-regex, preserving behavior.

Testing/Validation:
- Must pass golden corpus + shared e2e harness; targeted tests in git_safety_guard-ksk.2.3.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:09:02.330543605-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T17:03:37.809657953-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.2","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:09:02.331862623-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.2","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:09:08.094650438-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.2","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.1.4","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:20:41.659466769-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.2","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-64dc.5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T16:21:21.108815012-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}

-{"id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.3","title":"EnabledKeywordIndex: global keyword scan + pack prefilter","description":"Build a reusable keyword index to avoid repeated per-pack scanning.","notes":"Current flow: global quick-reject scans all keywords; then each pack re-scans its own keywords in both safe + destructive passes. This duplicates work.

Goal: build an index (Aho-Corasick + keyword→pack mapping) once per enabled pack set, then for each command: (1) quick-reject if no keyword in executable spans (same semantics as pack_aware_quick_reject), (2) compute a conservative candidate pack list from raw substring matches (must be a superset of current pack.might_match), and (3) only evaluate those packs in the same deterministic order.

Isomorphism constraints: quick-reject uses executable spans + word boundaries; candidate-pack filtering must NOT introduce false negatives relative to pack.might_match (substring). Empty keyword list remains conservative (no skipping). Sanitized view must be used whenever current pipeline does so.

Testing/Validation:
- Must pass golden corpus (git_safety_guard-ksk.1.2) and shared e2e harness (git_safety_guard-ksk.1.4) with verbose logs.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-ksk.3.1","title":"Design keyword index + word-boundary rules","description":"Specify data structures and exact matching semantics before coding.","notes":"Document: (a) how executable spans are derived, (b) how word-boundary is enforced for quick-reject, (c) how candidate pack sets are computed using conservative substring semantics (superset of pack.might_match), and (d) fallback behavior when no keywords configured or when sanitization returns Owned.

Explicitly call out: quick-reject and candidate-pack selection can use different matching strictness, as long as candidate selection is never more restrictive than the legacy pack.might_match.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-tlog","title":"Add detailed tracing and logging for heredoc detection","description":"# Tracing and Logging Implementation

## Objective

Add comprehensive tracing using the `tracing` crate to enable debugging and monitoring of heredoc detection.

## Dependencies to Add

```toml
[dependencies]
tracing = "0.1"
tracing-subscriber = { version = "0.3", features = ["env-filter"] }
```

## Log Levels

- **ERROR**: parsing failures, timeout exceeded
- **WARN**: fallback triggered, unknown language
- **INFO**: heredoc detected, command blocked/allowed
- **DEBUG**: pattern matches, timing details
- **TRACE**: full command text, AST details (redacted/truncated as needed)

## Instrumentation Points

### 1. Main Check Flow

```rust
#[tracing::instrument(skip(cmd), fields(cmd_len = cmd.len()))]
pub fn check_command(cmd: &str) -> CheckResult {
    let start = Instant::now();

    // Quick reject
    if global_quick_reject(cmd) {
        tracing::trace!("quick_reject: no relevant keywords");
        return CheckResult::allowed();
    }

    // Heredoc detection
    if might_contain_heredoc(cmd) {
        tracing::debug!("heredoc_detected: triggering Tier 2 analysis");
        if let Some(result) = check_heredoc(cmd) {
            tracing::info!(
                blocked = result.blocked,
                reason = ?result.reason,
                elapsed_us = start.elapsed().as_micros(),
                "heredoc_check_complete"
            );
            return result;
        }
    }

    // Pattern matching...
}
```

### 2. Heredoc Analysis

```rust
#[tracing::instrument(skip(cmd))]
fn check_heredoc(cmd: &str) -> Option<CheckResult> {
    let extraction_start = Instant::now();
    let heredoc = extract_heredoc(cmd)?;
    tracing::debug!(
        extraction_us = extraction_start.elapsed().as_micros(),
        content_len = heredoc.content.len(),
        "heredoc_extracted"
    );

    let lang = detect_language(&heredoc);
    tracing::debug!(language = ?lang, "language_detected");

    let parse_start = Instant::now();
    let matches = match_patterns(&heredoc.content, lang);
    tracing::debug!(
        parse_us = parse_start.elapsed().as_micros(),
        match_count = matches.len(),
        "patterns_checked"
    );

    // ...
}
```

### 3. Error Handling

```rust
match parse_with_ast_engine(&content, lang) {
    Ok(ast) => { /* ... */ }
    Err(e) => {
        tracing::warn!(
            error = %e,
            language = ?lang,
            "ast_parse_failed: falling back to allow"
        );
        return None;  // Fail-open (hook)
    }
}
```

## Environment Variable Control

```bash
# Enable heredoc tracing
RUST_LOG=dcg::heredoc=debug dcg check

# Enable timing info only
RUST_LOG=dcg=info dcg check

# Full trace (very verbose)
RUST_LOG=dcg=trace dcg check
```

## JSON Structured Logging

For machine parsing:

```rust
tracing_subscriber::fmt()
    .json()
    .with_env_filter(EnvFilter::from_default_env())
    .init();
```

## Performance impact

- Logging disabled: near-zero overhead (static checks)
- INFO/DEBUG: bounded overhead per event
- TRACE: may include serialization; should be opt-in and redacted

## Acceptance Criteria

- When tracing is disabled, heredoc path performance is not meaningfully impacted.
- When enabled, logs include:
  - tier timings (trigger/extract/parse/match)
  - language chosen (or unknown)
  - rule attribution for matches
  - explicit markers when fail-open occurs (timeout/parse error)
- Logging is safe-by-default:
  - no unbounded command/contents output
  - redaction/truncation available
- Unit tests can capture logs deterministically for at least one positive and one error path.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-tlp","title":"[PACK] messaging.rabbitmq - RabbitMQ Pack","description":"# RabbitMQ Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for RabbitMQ CLI and admin operations.

## Destructive Patterns
- `rabbitmqadmin delete queue`
- `rabbitmqadmin delete exchange`
- `rabbitmqadmin purge queue`
- `rabbitmqctl delete_vhost`
- `rabbitmqctl forget_cluster_node`
- `rabbitmqctl reset`
- `rabbitmqctl force_reset`

## Safe Patterns
- `rabbitmqadmin list`
- `rabbitmqadmin show`
- `rabbitmqctl status`
- `rabbitmqctl list_queues`
- `rabbitmqctl cluster_status`
- `rabbitmqctl report`

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: messaging.* (git_safety_guard-me0)
- Keywords: rabbitmqadmin, rabbitmqctl
- Note: purge is particularly dangerous (message loss)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Queue/exchange deletion blocked

## What to Do

Create a `codecov.yml` file in the repository root to configure Codecov's behavior for PR comments, coverage targets, and file ignores.

## Configuration Content

```yaml
# codecov.yml - Codecov configuration for DCG
# Docs: https://docs.codecov.com/docs/codecov-yaml

codecov:
  require_ci_to_pass: true

coverage:
  precision: 2
  round: down
  range: "60...100"
  
  status:
    project:
      default:
        # Match our CI-enforced threshold
        target: 70%
        threshold: 2%  # Allow 2% fluctuation without failing
        
    patch:
      default:
        # New code should have high coverage
        target: 80%
        threshold: 5%

comment:
  layout: "reach,diff,flags,tree,betaprofiling"
  behavior: default
  require_changes: true
  require_base: false
  require_head: true

ignore:
  - "tests/**"
  - "benches/**"
  - "fuzz/**"
  - "*.md"
  - "scripts/**"

flags:
  unittests:
    paths:
      - src/
    carryforward: true
```

## Configuration Explained

### Coverage Status
- **project target: 70%** - Matches our CI-enforced minimum
- **patch target: 80%** - New code in PRs should be well-tested
- **threshold: 2%** - Small fluctuations don't fail status checks

### Comment Layout
- **reach** - Shows lines covered
- **diff** - Shows coverage diff vs base
- **flags** - Shows coverage by flag (unittests)
- **tree** - Shows file-by-file breakdown
- **betaprofiling** - Shows performance impact (beta feature)

### Ignore Patterns
We exclude test/bench/fuzz directories since:
- Test code doesn't need coverage (it IS the coverage)
- Benchmark code is performance testing, not unit testing
- Fuzz targets are input generators

### Flags
The `unittests` flag lets us track unit test coverage separately from integration tests if we add more test suites later.

## Why These Settings

1. **Targets match CI** - Codecov targets align with our enforced thresholds
2. **PR comments enabled** - Developers see coverage impact immediately
3. **Ignores match llvm-cov** - Same exclusions as our cargo llvm-cov config
4. **Carryforward** - If tests don't run, use previous coverage data

## Files to Create

- `codecov.yml` (in repo root)

- [ ] codecov.yml created in repo root
- [ ] Project target set to 70%
- [ ] Patch target set to 80%
- [ ] Ignore patterns match our llvm-cov config

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-uau","title":"Define Bash/Shell destructive patterns for heredoc scanning","description":"## Objective

Define how to detect destructive shell operations inside *shell* heredoc bodies with high signal and low false positives.

## Key insight

For shell heredocs, we should avoid inventing a second “pattern language” if we can reuse the existing dcg evaluator.

Preferred approach:

1. Parse the heredoc body as shell (tree-sitter-bash).
2. Extract candidate simple-command invocations (command + args).
3. Reconstruct a normalized command string.
4. Feed each extracted command into the existing evaluator (packs + overrides + severity policy).

This keeps all destructive knowledge in one place (packs) and reduces drift.

## What to extract (v1)

- Simple commands: `rm -rf ...`, `git reset --hard ...`, `docker system prune`, etc.
- Pipe targets: right-hand side of `|` (executed)
- Command substitutions: `$(...)` / backticks (executed)

## What NOT to treat as executed (avoid false positives)

- comments
- string literals printed with `echo`/`printf`
- here-doc delimiters themselves

## Severity guidance

- Direct matches to catastrophic pack rules → `Error` (deny by default)
- Ambiguous/dynamic constructs (variable-built commands) → `Warning` at most unless payload is confidently destructive

## Testing requirements

- Positive fixtures (must detect):
  - `rm -rf /` (or other catastrophic)
  - `git reset --hard`
  - `git clean -fd`
  - `docker system prune` (if pack enabled)
- Negative fixtures (must not flag):
  - `echo "rm -rf"`
  - comments mentioning dangerous commands
  - harmless cleanup under `/tmp` (if policy allows)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Defines the extraction→evaluation approach clearly enough to implement without re-reading external plans.
- Produces a fixture list that covers:
  - executed vs data-only contexts
  - pipes and command substitution
- Avoids introducing new destructive-rule definitions for shell when existing packs already encode them.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-unit","title":"Create comprehensive unit test suite for heredoc detection","description":"# Unit Test Suite for Heredoc Detection

## Objective

Create thorough unit tests for each component of the heredoc detection system.

## Test Modules

### 1. Tier 1 Regex Tests (`tests/tier1_regex.rs`)

```rust
mod tier1_regex_tests {
    use super::*;
    
    #[test]
    fn test_bash_heredoc_variants() {
        // Standard heredoc
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("cat << EOF"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("cat <<EOF"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("cat << 'EOF'"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("cat << \"EOF\""));
        
        // Indented heredoc
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("cat <<- EOF"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("cat <<-EOF"));
        
        // Here-string
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("cat <<< 'hello'"));
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn test_inline_script_flags() {
        // Python
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("python -c 'print(1)'"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("python3 -c 'print(1)'"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("python -e 'print(1)'")); // -e not valid but we catch
        
        // Node.js
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("node -e 'console.log(1)'"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("node -p 'process.version'"));
        
        // Ruby
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("ruby -e 'puts 1'"));
        
        // Perl
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("perl -e 'print 1'"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("perl -E 'say 1'"));
        
        // Shell
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("bash -c 'echo hi'"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("sh -c 'echo hi'"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("zsh -c 'echo hi'"));
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn test_pipe_to_interpreter() {
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("curl http://x.com | bash"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("cat script.py | python3"));
        assert!(might_contain_heredoc("echo code | node"));
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn test_negative_cases() {
        assert!(!might_contain_heredoc("git status"));
        assert!(!might_contain_heredoc("cargo build"));
        assert!(!might_contain_heredoc("ls -la"));
        assert!(!might_contain_heredoc("cat file.txt"));
        assert!(!might_contain_heredoc("grep pattern file"));
    }
}
```

### 2. Heredoc Extraction Tests (`tests/extraction.rs`)

```rust
mod extraction_tests {
    #[test]
    fn test_extract_simple_heredoc() {
        let cmd = "python3 << 'EOF'\nprint('hello')\nEOF";
        let heredoc = extract_heredoc(cmd).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(heredoc.content, "print('hello')\n");
        assert_eq!(heredoc.delimiter, "EOF");
        assert!(heredoc.quoted);
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn test_extract_indented_heredoc() {
        let cmd = "bash <<- 'END'\n\techo hello\n\techo world\n\tEND";
        let heredoc = extract_heredoc(cmd).unwrap();
        // <<- strips leading tabs
        assert_eq!(heredoc.content, "echo hello\necho world\n");
    }
    
    #[test]
    fn test_extract_inline_script() {
        let cmd = "python3 -c 'import os; os.system(\"ls\")'";
        let heredoc = extract_heredoc(cmd).unwrap();
        assert_eq!(heredoc.content, "import os; os.system(\"ls\")");
    }
}
```

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-xld","title":"Epic: CI/CD Maturity & Observability Enhancements","description":"# Epic: CI/CD Maturity & Observability Enhancements

## Background & Context

This epic addresses gaps identified in a comprehensive comparison between DCG's CI/CD setup and CASS (coding_agent_session_search). While DCG already has several advanced CI features (fuzz testing, enforced coverage thresholds, release automation, scheduled deep testing, performance budgets), there are four key areas where CASS has capabilities that would benefit DCG.

## Strategic Goals

DCG is a security-critical tool that guards against destructive commands in AI coding workflows. As such, it requires:

1. **Maximum reliability** - Any bug could either block legitimate work (false positive) or allow destructive commands (false negative)
2. **Performance stability** - Every Bash command passes through DCG, so performance regressions directly impact user experience
3. **Security hardening** - As a security tool, DCG must maintain the highest code quality standards
4. **Maintainability** - Dependencies must stay current to avoid security vulnerabilities

## Gap Analysis (DCG vs CASS)

| Feature | DCG Status | CASS Status | Priority |
|---------|------------|-------------|----------|
| Codecov cloud integration | ❌ Missing | ✅ Has | P2 |
| Memory leak tests | ❌ Missing | ✅ Has | P2 |
| UBS static analysis | ❌ Missing | ✅ Has | P3 |
| Dependabot config | ❌ Missing | ❌ Missing | P3 |

## Why These Matter for DCG

### 1. Codecov Cloud Integration
- **Current state:** Coverage artifacts stored locally, no trend tracking
- **Problem:** Can't see coverage trends over time, no PR coverage diff comments
- **Solution:** Upload to Codecov for historical tracking and PR integration
- **DCG-specific benefit:** Critical paths (evaluator.rs, hook.rs) need visibility

### 2. Memory Leak Detection
- **Current state:** No memory testing
- **Problem:** DCG runs as a hook on EVERY command - memory leaks accumulate
- **Solution:** Add dedicated memory tests with leak detection
- **DCG-specific benefit:** Long Claude Code sessions = thousands of hook invocations

### 3. UBS (Ultimate Bug Scanner) Integration
- **Current state:** Only clippy for static analysis
- **Problem:** clippy misses some bug categories UBS catches
- **Solution:** Run UBS on changed files in CI
- **DCG-specific benefit:** Security tool needs defense in depth

### 4. Dependabot Configuration
- **Current state:** Manual dependency updates
- **Problem:** Stale dependencies = security vulnerabilities
- **Solution:** Automated PRs for Cargo and GitHub Actions updates
- **DCG-specific benefit:** fancy-regex, serde_json, etc. need security patches

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Coverage trends visible in Codecov dashboard
- [ ] PR comments show coverage diff
- [ ] Memory tests run in CI, detect leaks
- [ ] UBS runs on PRs, blocks on critical findings
- [ ] Dependabot creates PRs for outdated dependencies
- [ ] All new CI jobs documented in AGENTS.md

## Dependencies

This epic has no blockers - all work can proceed independently.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-y4j","title":"Implement heredoc content extraction","description":"# Implement Heredoc Content Extraction

## Objective

Extract the actual script content from detected heredocs / inline scripts for scanning.

## Background

Once we detect a heredoc is present, we need to accurately extract its content. This is more complex than detection because we must:

1. Identify the delimiter
2. Find the matching end delimiter
3. Handle quoting and expansion rules
4. Preserve or strip indentation as appropriate

## Implementation Details

### Heredoc extraction logic

```rust
pub struct HeredocContent {
    /// The extracted script content
    pub content: String,
    /// Detected or inferred language
    pub language: Option<Language>,
    /// Original delimiter used
    pub delimiter: String,
    /// Whether the delimiter was quoted (no expansion)
    pub quoted: bool,
    /// Byte offset in original command
    pub start_offset: usize,
    pub end_offset: usize,
}

pub fn extract_heredoc(cmd: &str) -> Option<HeredocContent> {
    // 1. Find heredoc start pattern
    // 2. Extract delimiter (handle quoting)
    // 3. Find matching end delimiter
    // 4. Extract content between
    // 5. Apply indentation rules for <<- / <<~
}
```

### Delimiter handling

- Unquoted: EOF, END, SCRIPT
- Single-quoted: `'EOF'`, `'END'`
- Double-quoted: `"EOF"`, `"END"`

### Indentation rules

- Standard `<<`: preserve all indentation
- Tab-stripped `<<-`: remove leading tabs from content AND delimiter line
- Zsh `<<~`: remove common leading whitespace (decide v1 support explicitly)

### Edge cases

- multiple heredocs in one command
- heredocs with pipes
- empty heredocs
- malformed heredocs (missing terminator)

### Inline script extraction (non-heredoc)

Also handle `-c`/`-e` style inline scripts:

- `python -c "import os; os.system('...')"`
- `node -e "require('fs').rmSync('/', { recursive: true })"`

## Performance + safety constraints

- Extraction must be bounded by a max byte limit (ties into global limits).
- On malformed or ambiguous input in hook mode: fail-open and emit diagnostic trace/log.

## Testing

- delimiter quoting variants
- `<<`, `<<-`, (optional) `<<~`
- inline `-c`/`-e` extraction
- malformed heredocs (no crash)
- multiple heredocs

## Acceptance Criteria

- Extracts correct content for all heredoc forms explicitly supported by `git_safety_guard-o15`.
- Handles malformed input without panicking; hook mode defaults to allow with a diagnostic.
- Honors size limits (truncates/aborts extraction in a defined way) and does not allocate unboundedly.
- Provides stable offsets/line mapping sufficient for explain output (match spans / context windows).

-{"id":"git_safety_guard-ypb","title":"Add Codecov upload step to CI coverage job","description":"# Task: Add Codecov Upload to CI Coverage Job

## What to Do

Modify `.github/workflows/ci.yml` to upload coverage data to Codecov after generating the LCOV report.

## Current State

The coverage job currently:
1. Runs tests with coverage instrumentation
2. Generates lcov.info and coverage-summary.txt
3. Checks coverage thresholds (enforced)
4. Uploads artifacts locally

## Changes Required

Add this step after "Generate coverage reports" and before "Check coverage thresholds":

```yaml
- name: Upload coverage to Codecov
  uses: codecov/codecov-action@v4
  with:
    files: lcov.info
    fail_ci_if_error: false
    verbose: true
    name: dcg-coverage
    flags: unittests
  env:
    CODECOV_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.CODECOV_TOKEN }}

- name: Codecov upload status
  if: always()
  run: |
    echo "## Codecov Upload" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    if [ -f lcov.info ]; then
      lines=$(wc -l < lcov.info)
      echo "- Coverage file: lcov.info ($lines lines)" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    else
      echo "- ⚠️ Coverage file not found" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    fi
    echo "- Upload: Attempted (check Codecov dashboard for status)" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "Dashboard: https://codecov.io/gh/Dicklesworthstone/destructive_command_guard" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
```

## Why fail_ci_if_error: false

Codecov upload can fail for transient reasons:
- Network issues
- Codecov service outage
- Rate limiting

Our local threshold enforcement is the gate. Codecov is additive visibility, not a blocker.

## Why verbose: true

Verbose output helps debug upload issues:
- Shows file being uploaded
- Shows upload progress
- Shows any errors with context

## Why flags: unittests

Flags allow tracking coverage by test suite:
- Currently just "unittests" 
- Could add "integration" or "e2e" later
- Codecov shows per-flag coverage in dashboard

## Token Considerations

For public repos, CODECOV_TOKEN is optional but recommended:
- More reliable uploads (no rate limiting)
- Required for PR comments from forks
- Add to GitHub repo Settings > Secrets

## Testing

1. Push change to a branch
2. Check CI logs for upload step output
3. Verify Codecov dashboard shows new upload
4. Check GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY shows upload status

## Files to Modify

- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (coverage job, ~line 96-125)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] codecov-action@v4 step added to coverage job
- [ ] Uses lcov.info file
- [ ] fail_ci_if_error set to false
- [ ] verbose: true for debugging
- [ ] flags: unittests for categorization
- [ ] Status logged to GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
- [ ] Dashboard link in summary
- [ ] CI passes with new step

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-yza","title":"Epic: ast-grep Integration for Heredoc Detection","description":"# Epic: ast-grep Integration for Heredoc Detection

## Problem Statement

AI coding agents sometimes attempt to bypass destructive command guards by embedding dangerous commands inside heredoc scripts. The current dcg implementation only examines the top-level command, missing destructive patterns hidden within inline scripts.

### Example Attack Vectors

An attacker might use Python, Bash, Node.js, or other language heredocs to embed dangerous operations that slip past the quick-reject filter. The top-level command appears benign (e.g., "python3 << EOF") while the heredoc body contains destructive operations.

## Solution Overview

Integrate ast-grep (or tree-sitter directly) to:
1. Detect heredoc patterns in commands
2. Extract embedded script content
3. Parse the script according to its detected language
4. Check for destructive patterns within the parsed AST
5. Block if dangerous patterns are found

## Why ast-grep?

ast-grep uses tree-sitter for parsing, providing:
- **Structural awareness**: Understands code structure, not just text patterns
- **Language support**: Handles Python, Bash, JavaScript, TypeScript, Ruby, Perl, and many more
- **Pattern matching**: Powerful AST pattern matching syntax
- **Battle-tested**: Used in production for large-scale code search and refactoring

## Key Technical Challenges

1. **Heredoc Syntax Variants**: Many forms exist (<<, <<-, <<<, <<~, quoted vs unquoted delimiters)
2. **Language Detection**: Must infer language from command prefix, shebang, or heuristics
3. **Performance**: Every command passes through dcg; parsing must be fast
4. **Pattern Library**: Need comprehensive patterns per language for destructive operations
5. **Obfuscation**: Attackers might use encoding, string concatenation, or indirect execution

## Success Criteria

- Detect and block heredoc-embedded destructive commands
- Minimal latency impact (under 10ms for heredoc detection, under 50ms for full parsing)
- Support Python, Bash, JavaScript/TypeScript, Ruby, Perl at minimum
- Configurable via pack system (heredoc scanning can be enabled/disabled)
- Comprehensive test coverage for bypass attempts

## Architecture Decision

We will evaluate two integration approaches:
1. **External binary**: Call ast-grep CLI and parse JSON output
2. **Library integration**: Use tree-sitter Rust bindings directly

The decision will be made in the research phase based on:
- Performance benchmarks
- Dependency complexity
- Maintenance burden
- Pattern expressiveness

## Out of Scope (for initial implementation)

- Scanning files referenced in commands (e.g., "bash script.sh")
- Deep obfuscation detection (base64, rot13, etc.)
- Network-based command retrieval ("curl ... | bash" style)
- Recursive heredoc nesting","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-07T18:28:08.502051689-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-09T13:16:27.497754754-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-09T13:16:27.497754754-05:00","close_reason":"Completed: heredoc scanning + ast-grep integration delivered; all child beads closed.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-yza","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-t8x","type":"relates-to","created_at":"2026-01-07T21:53:37.07782574-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","title":"[PACK] platform.gitlab - GitLab Platform Pack","description":"# GitLab Platform Pack (Non-CI Operations)

## Scope Clarification
This pack covers GitLab **platform-wide** operations that are NOT covered by `cicd.gitlab_ci`:
- Project/repository management
- Releases
- Protected branches
- Webhooks
- Group management

**The `cicd.gitlab_ci` pack covers:**
- CI/CD variables
- Pipelines
- Runners

## Destructive Patterns (Platform-specific)

### Project Operations
- `glab repo delete` - Project deletion
- `glab repo archive` - Archive project

### Release Operations
- `glab release delete` - Deletes releases

### API Operations
- `glab api -X DELETE projects/` - Project deletion
- `glab api -X DELETE .../protected_branches/` - Remove protection
- `glab api -X DELETE .../hooks/` - Webhook deletion

### Admin Operations (self-hosted)
- `gitlab-rails runner` with destructive code
- `gitlab-rake` destructive maintenance tasks

## Safe Patterns
- `glab repo view`, `glab repo list`, `glab repo clone`
- `glab mr list`, `glab mr view`
- `glab issue list`, `glab issue view`
- `glab api` with GET method

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: platform.gitlab (git_safety_guard-h5j)
- Keywords: glab, gitlab-rails, gitlab-rake
- IMPORTANT: Do NOT duplicate patterns from cicd.gitlab_ci

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests in `src/packs/platform/gitlab.rs`
- [ ] E2E tests in `scripts/e2e_tests/platform_gitlab.txt`
- [ ] Verify no overlap with cicd.gitlab_ci patterns
- [ ] Test project/release operations
- [ ] Performance < 500µs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Project deletion blocked
- [ ] Release deletion blocked
- [ ] Protected branch removal blocked
- [ ] No duplicate patterns with cicd.gitlab_ci
- [ ] Completion checklist passed","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-09T14:33:47.879121793-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T18:00:42.277384353-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T18:00:42.277384353-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented platform.gitlab pack + unit tests + added E2E coverage in scripts/e2e_test.sh","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:38.943656825-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-i79","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:38.988017189-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ct5s","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:39.031117693-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-jdb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:39.074253284-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-e7j","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:39.116035858-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-l968","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:07:39.157638945-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qxc7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:11.553226423-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-z2q","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-6ozg","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T17:47:11.598241766-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
